The Essence of Learning

Intro

It's been a while since I shared something about learning experiences, so let's write one about the essence of learning.
the-essence-of-learning
The essence of learning
First — have you ever had this thought:
pre
language-txt shiki shiki-themes github-dark-high-contrast
How should I learn xxxx?
txt
code
span
line
Or:
Which one should I pick to learn?
I probably hear or see lines like these two or three times a week.

Before we get into it, I want to thank this post's sponsors and supporters. Without them I wouldn't be writing this. The two are:
.
.
.
.
.
.
.
OK, this post has no sponsors. Just kidding.
Feel a bit lighter? I hope you read this with some ease, not in anxiety and pain. That's why I joked.
So what is learning? A lot of people will tell you learning is painful.
Oh — you might push back: "I don't find learning painful at all, I love learning!"
OK, but if I told you to read into a completely unfamiliar domain and master it within a limited time, would you still find it fun?
Picture this: you have zero math background, and suddenly you have two days to learn calculus, trig, and all sorts of math knowledge — would that hurt?
Or you have a short timeframe to master three foreign languages — German, French, Arabic, whatever — that's painful, right?
is-the-essence-of-learning-pain
Is the essence of learning pain?
OK, so is the essence of learning pain? No — the essence of learning isn't pain, it's growth.

What does that mean? Think back to school. You picked up Chinese, English, math, science, social studies all pretty easily. Why so easily? Because you could choose not to learn — you'd just fail the final (lol).
Why don't we feel the pain of learning as strongly back in school as we do as adults?
The reason is the environment.

After you enter society, you have to work to feed yourself, your kids, pay for a car, buy a house, socialize — endless stuff. To do all that, you have to make money. To improve your life quality even a notch, you have to master specific knowledge in a short timeframe — three months, six months — in fields you're not familiar with.
You see the issue, right?
The reason learning feels painful is that you're being
strong
forced to learn specific knowledge within a limited time
. So you start feeling pain and increasing anxiety.
The reason learning feels painful is being forced by life and environment — not learning itself.
How do you solve "learning is painful"? Simply put: learn through interest.
No matter your age, you've played a video game, right?
Think about it — why can you spend a long time on a game you've just touched, even when you hit walls? You keep going and try to clear levels?
Take Switch Zelda. I'm a controller idiot — I literally can't operate it. Took me almost two weeks to get used to a 3D camera. Was that process painful? Yes, immensely.
I had no idea how to control the camera and got killed by monsters constantly (sigh).
But I didn't quit. Simple reason: I thought the game was fun (I'm not saying being killed was fun — I'm not into masochism).

When you're interested in something, even if the screen is full of unfamiliar foreign text and the controls are super hard, you'll learn how to operate and play. So what you should really think about is "how to make learning feel interesting".
Sounds like smartass advice, right? I know.

But I mean it. If you don't work to find interest, what's left is just pain. Even if you're learning something for money, you should cultivate interest. Otherwise your skill tree branches everywhere without depth — you learn a bit here and a bit there, never mastering anything, and eventually feel pain.

That's why many language books for kids use "games" to teach. Treat learning like playing a game and you'll have momentum, because it'll feel interesting. You'll get the "wow~ cool, you can do that?" feeling.
This post isn't going to tell you how to turn what you're learning into a game — everyone learns differently. I just want you to understand the essence of learning.
what-should-i-learn
What should I learn?
Now that we've covered the essence, let me bring it back to a common frontend engineer situation.
I get asked all the time: "Ray, should I learn Vue?", "I see more job openings for React — should I switch to that?" If you're about to start job hunting, I'd say
stop this mode of thinking
. If you're still in learning mode, keep going. This isn't what you need to worry about right now. Someone who can't crawl shouldn't try to run.
Why do I tell job-hunters to stop thinking this way? Imagine you learned React, then look at openings and they're full of Vue and Angular. Do you go learn Vue or Angular? Spend more time?

OK, say you did spend time on Vue or Angular. Then you come back to the market and find React is the new mainstream. Wouldn't you feel anxious — like there's no end to this? Engineering really is endless learning. But if you keep chasing whatever has more openings...
I guarantee you'll never finish.
You'll get more and more anxious — "knowledge anxiety" territory.
So what should you do? Find a job that's willing to take you in first, then learn based on what the job needs.

But what if the company's tech doesn't match what you've learned? Companies usually give time to learn and pick it up — if you cleared the interview, you have the baseline they need. Getting handed a gun on the actual battlefield is a clearer goal than sitting in a classroom researching guns. You won't be in constant knowledge anxiety either.

One core point I want you to internalize: whether you learn Vue, React, or Angular — they're all built on JavaScript. Rather than learning to wield a framework, build a rock-solid foundation. The three skills frontend engineers must have — CSS, HTML, JavaScript — taken to the top.
When those three are solid, do you think any framework is hard to learn? No — what's left is just reading docs.

So don't fall into the weird mindset of "more openings for framework X". If you start with that mindset, you'll easily get hostage'd by the framework — frameworks update and evolve. If you only use a framework to find work, you can't escape it. When the framework stops being maintained, you go down with it. With strong fundamentals, that's a different story.
So please build the fundamentals well — it'll help more.
